summary: The q3 dataset shows uniform activity across all ten problems (q1-q10), with each carrying exactly 100 issues and no differentiating proof-status metadata available. The lack of variance and absence of resolution or categorization data makes it impossible to identify true traction, solvability, or progress from the current context. Improved instrumentation is required before reliable prioritization can be performed.
problems: All problems (q1-q10) report an identical count of 100 issues, indicating either placeholder/default data or a lack of granular tracking.
No proof-status breakdown (e.g., open vs. resolved, verified vs. unverified) is provided, preventing assessment of actual progress.
No per-problem activity, traction, or recency signals are available to distinguish high-momentum problems from stalled ones.
Common issue patterns cannot be extracted because issue content and categorization metadata are missing.
Solvability estimates cannot be made due to absence of difficulty, dependency, or partial-progress indicators.
highlights: Complete coverage: all ten problems are tracked and present in the dataset with no missing entries.
Consistent issue volume provides a clean baseline for measuring future divergence once richer metadata is added.
The uniform structure suggests the tracking pipeline is operational and ready for enhanced instrumentation.

suggested_todos.title: Instrument issue tracker to record proof status (open/resolved/verified) per problem
Add issue categorization and tagging to surface common failure patterns across q1-q10
Capture per-problem activity and recency metrics to identify genuine traction
Define a solvability/difficulty scoring rubric to enable next-week prioritization
Audit why all problems show an identical count of 100 issues to rule out placeholder data
